sevenDayForecast.headline: Tropical Cyclone Koji has developed near the northeast Queensland coast.
points: Forecast Track Maps and Tropical Cyclone Advices are being issued for Tropical Cyclone Koji (12U) and should be referred to for more information. A Severe Weather Warning and Flood Watches and Warnings are also current.
Koji lies to the east of Innisfail and is moving generally south towards the northeast Queensland coast.
Koji is expected to cross the coast early Sunday morning, moving inland and weakening below a tropical cyclone during Sunday.

In the longer term there is a risk that a weakened Koji moves to the northwest, over Gulf of Carpentaria waters. If this occurs there is a Low risk of redeveloping into a tropical cyclone from Friday.

trackForecast.headline: Tropical Cyclone Koji (12U) has developed, east of Innisfail, impacts likely from early evening.
trackForecast.discussion:
Tropical Cyclone Koji (12U) has developed in the Coral Sea, currently a category 2 system that lies offshore east of Innisfail. Koji is moving south towards the northeast Queensland coast, and likely to cross the coast between Townsville and Bowen early Sunday.

Observations at offshore reefs and Willis Island show that gales extend into three quadrants, and consequently Koji has been named. Koji is forecast to remain a category 2 until it interacts with land during early Sunday morning.

The Abbott Point area is currently under a Tropical Cyclone Warning. Gales with damaging wind gusts to 100 km/h are likely from this evening. Destructive wind gusts are possible later tonight or early Sunday morning if 12U tracks any further south than the current forecast. Heavy to intense rainfall is also possible in the area tonight and on Sunday.

After crossing the coast, the winds of the system will quickly weaken and the risk of gales should rapidly ease. Heavy rain may continue, however.
trackForecast.discussionUpperBound: The upper bound is based on the system slowly developing until landfall, rather than maintaining intensity as is currently forecast, and being slower to reach the coast thus having more time to develop.
